title: Median income or consumption per day vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Median income or consumption per day vs. GDP per capita, 2024
This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

The scatter plot maps the relationship between national economic output and the financial reality of the typical citizen, plotting GDP per capita on the horizontal axis against median income or consumption per day on the vertical axis. Both axes utilize a logarithmic scale measured in international dollars at 2021 prices, allowing for the comparison of nations across vast economic disparities. The data points form a distinct positive correlation, where higher national income per person generally coincides with higher median daily income for individuals. At the lower end of the distribution, countries such as Burundi, the Democratic Republic of Congo, and Madagascar cluster in the bottom-left quadrant, showing GDP per capita below $2,000 and median daily income or consumption under $2. Moving toward the middle of the chart, populous Asian nations including India, Pakistan, and Indonesia occupy the center, with GDP per capita ranging between $5,000 and $10,000 and median daily figures hovering between $5 and $10. China and Vietnam appear slightly higher, with median daily incomes approaching or exceeding $15. In the upper-right section, high-income economies demonstrate the strongest values; the United States sits at the extreme top-right with a GDP per capita exceeding $50,000 and a median daily income above $50. Japan and European nations also populate this high-value region. Circle sizes correspond to population, making India and China visually dominant in the middle range. The visual arrangement shows that while median income rises with GDP per capita, the slope of this relationship varies across the income spectrum.
